pubmed_links: [van Bon et al. (2016) Disruptive de novo mutations of DYRK1A lead to a syndromic form of autism and ID](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/25707398/);[Kurtz-Nelson et al. (2023) Characterizing the autism spectrum phenotype in DYRK1A-related syndrome](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/37497568/);[Neuhaus et al. (2024) Shared and divergent mental health characteristics of ADNP-, CHD8- and DYRK1A-related neurodevelopmental conditions](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/38622540/)
summary: DYRK1A encodes a dual-specificity kinase critical for brain development, and heterozygous loss-of-function mutations cause a well-characterized syndrome featuring microcephaly, intellectual disability, and autism spectrum disorder. Accounting for 0.1–0.5% of individuals with ID and/or ASD, DYRK1A-related syndrome is one of the more frequent monogenic causes of neurodevelopmental disability. (well-established)
